title: Gruyere gougeres
subtitle: Savoury choux puffs with melted Gruyere
excerpt: Gougeres are savoury choux pastry puffs flavoured generously with Gruyere and a little mustard. They bake up hollow and light, with a pronounced cheese flavour and a crisp, dry shell. Serve warm.
sourceNotes: Gougeres are from Burgundy, France, where they are traditionally served with wine at the start of a meal. The form of cheese-flavoured choux puffs is documented in Burgundian cooking from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), gives gougeres in the hors d'oeuvre section using Gruyere or Parmesan. The same preparation appears in Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960), as a characteristic Burgundian speciality. Comte can replace Gruyere.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen in a 200°C oven for 5 to 6 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Bake in batches; the paste keeps for 2 hours covered at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best served warm from the oven. They soften as they cool but can be reheated successfully.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked dough made by heating flour, butter, and water together, then beating in eggs. The paste is piped or spooned and baked: steam from the high moisture content puffs the pastry up, creating hollow, light shells. For gougeres, cheese is beaten into the paste after the eggs.

content.text: with Gruyere beaten in after the eggs. The cheese melts into the paste and flavours the shell throughout, not just on the surface. Do not open the oven door in the first 20 minutes: the choux needs constant heat to rise and set the walls. Opening the oven causes them to collapse. They are done when the shells are deep amber and feel firm when gently pressed.

items.prepNote: finely grated, plus extra for topping

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Combine the water, milk, butter, and salt in a medium saucepan. Bring to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat and add the sifted flour all at once. Stir vigorously until the mixture forms a smooth ball that pulls away from the sides of the pan. Return to low heat for 1 minute to dry the paste. Transfer to a large bowl and cool for 3 minutes. Beat in the eggs one at a time with a wooden spoon or electric mixer; the paste will look split at first but comes together with each addition. Beat in the Gruyere, mustard, and pepper.

Transfer the paste to a piping bag fitted with a 1.5 cm plain nozzle, or use two spoons, to portion mounds about 4 cm across onto the prepared trays, spaced 4 cm apart. Scatter a little extra Gruyere over each one. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes, without opening the oven, until deep amber, puffed, and firm when pressed. Cool on a rack for 5 minutes and serve warm.
